AEZ Land Type Soil Texture Drainage Recession Moisture

CEGIS · RDT Division — GIS Exercise 01

Half the Year,
Underwater.

Sylhet and Sunamganj sit at the bottom of a bowl. Monsoon floods fill it every year, and for months the only crop that survives is boro rice, planted after the water pulls back. Six map layers, built in ArcGIS Pro, trace why the land behaves this way.

7Districts mapped
~2.0M haHaor region extent
6Thematic layers
1Crop, most of the basin
Scroll — six layers follow

Why these six layers

One Wetland, Read Six Ways

The Haor Region covers most of Sylhet and Sunamganj, in the country's northeast. Deep monsoon flooding submerges it for months at a stretch, and the farming calendar bends entirely around when the water arrives and when it leaves.

To map what that actually means on the ground, six physical layers were built in ArcGIS Pro from the region's soil and AEZ shapefiles: agro-ecological zone, land type, soil texture, drainage, surface water recession, and soil moisture. Read together, they explain what can be grown, where, and for how long.

Attribute tables were checked first for missing or inconsistent values. Anywhere data was absent, it was tagged No Data rather than dropped, so every map covers the same footprint instead of leaving gaps.

Field note — a shapefile with no projection

The soil information layer arrived with its coordinate system undefined — no .prj attached. Before any clipping or dissolving could happen, Define Projection assigned Gulshan_303_Bangladesh_TM, built on the D Gulshan 303 datum and a Transverse Mercator projection.

Skip that step and every area figure on every map below comes out wrong. It's the kind of fix that's invisible when it works and obvious when it doesn't.

(a) Agro-Ecological Zones

Eleven Zones, Same Flood Line

45.6%

Sylhet Basin + Eastern Surma–Kusiyara Floodplain — deep flood, boro rice only

Eleven agro-ecological zones cover 1,944,041 ha. The east is split almost evenly between Sylhet Basin (22.9%) and the Eastern Surma-Kusiyara Floodplain (22.7%) — both flood deep enough that boro rice is the only crop that fits.

Move west into the Old Brahmaputra Floodplain and the picture changes. It covers 9.9% of the region, floods less, and gives farmers more to choose from.

  1. Dissolved the soil shapefile by district name into the seven Haor boundaries
  2. Used that boundary to clip the AEZ layer to the Haor extent
  3. Dissolved the clipped AEZ by region name to separate each zone
Fig. A — Agro-ecological regionsArcGIS Pro · Deboneel Partho

(b) Land Type

More Than Half the Region Sits Low

51.9%

Lowland + Medium Lowland + Very Lowland, combined

1,969,897 ha across eight land type classes. Lowland alone is the largest single class at 22.2% (437,921 ha), running through Sunamganj, Habiganj, and Sylhet. Add Medium Lowland (18.8%) and Very Lowland (10.9%) and just over half the region falls where flood depth sets the calendar.

Highland and Medium Highland together hold 36.1%, mostly at the edges — Netrokona, Kishoreganj, and the Maulvibazar fringe.

  1. Assigned No Data to missing land type values
  2. Field Calculator: !LANDTYPE!.split("/")[0] to strip trailing text
  3. Dissolved by land type, calculated area, derived percentages
Fig. B — Land type classificationArcGIS Pro · Deboneel Partho

(c) Top Soil Texture

Clay Underfoot, Almost Everywhere

68.2%

Clay Loam + Clay, combined — fine, slow-draining soil

Across 1,969,898 ha and ten texture classes, Clay Loam alone covers 39.2% (771,935 ha), and Clay adds another 29.0% (571,447 ha). That's fine, water-retentive ground that floods easily and dries slowly, concentrated through the central and eastern basin.

Loam (18.1%) sits mostly on the western fringe. Everything else — sandy loam, peat, muck, char — adds up to under 2% of the region.

  1. Assigned No Data to missing texture values
  2. Field Calculator: !TEXTURE!.split("/")[0] to strip trailing text
  3. Dissolved by texture, calculated area, derived percentages
Fig. C — Soil textureArcGIS Pro · Deboneel Partho

(d) Drainage Characteristics

41% of the Ground Barely Drains

41.4%

Poorly Drained — the single largest drainage class

Across 2,628,271 ha and six drainage classes, Poorly Drained takes the biggest share at 41.4% (1,087,214 ha), concentrated through Sunamganj, Habiganj, and Kishoreganj. Char land — low river deposits flooded every season — adds another 25.1% (659,031 ha).

Add Very Poorly Drained (5.5%) and close to half the region holds water far longer than an ordinary floodplain. Excessively drained ground does exist, just 4.4%, tucked into the hill fringes of Maulvibazar and Sylhet.

  1. Assigned No Data to missing drainage values
  2. Field Calculator: !DRAINAGE!.split("/")[0] to strip trailing text
  3. Dissolved by drainage class, calculated area, derived percentages
Fig. D — Drainage characteristicsArcGIS Pro · Deboneel Partho

(e) Surface Water Recession

The Water Leaves Late, If It Leaves at All

26.0%

Late recession — water lingers into the dry season

Recession timing splits into six classes across 1,969,898 ha. Late recession covers the most ground, 26.0% (512,960 ha), right over the deepest basins in Sunamganj and Habiganj. Very Late adds another 7.6% in those same cores.

At the other end, Extremely Early recession covers 22.5% of the region — the elevated fringe, where the ground sheds water fast. The two extremes sit right next to each other on the map, separated by elevation as much as by distance.

  1. Assigned No Data to missing recession values
  2. Field Calculator: !RECESSION!.split("/")[0] to strip trailing text
  3. Dissolved by recession class, calculated area, derived percentages
Fig. E — Surface water recessionArcGIS Pro · Deboneel Partho

(f) Soil Moisture

Where the Dry Season Actually Bites

41.1%

Low moisture — northern and eastern fringe

Soil moisture only needed three classes to cover 1,969,898 ha. Low moisture dominates at 41.1% (809,896 ha) along the northern and eastern fringe — ground that drains early and dries out fast. Medium moisture (29.3%) sits in the transitional middle, through Habiganj and Kishoreganj.

High moisture (10.9%) covers the least area, but it sits exactly where it matters: the deep basin of Sunamganj, where waterlogging outlasts everything else on the map.

  1. Assigned No Data to missing moisture values
  2. Field Calculator: !MOISTURE!.split("/")[0] to strip trailing text
  3. Dissolved by moisture class, calculated area, derived percentages
Fig. F — Soil moistureArcGIS Pro · Deboneel Partho

Putting the six together

Water Calls the Shots

46%+ poorly / very poorly drained 68% clay or clay loam 26% late recession 52% lowland or lower

Stack the six layers and one picture comes through. The soils are fine-textured and slow to drain. Water recedes late across the core basin. Dry-season moisture runs out fast almost everywhere except the deepest ground, where it barely runs out at all. Over 46% of the region is poorly or very poorly drained, and that single number explains most of what farmers here can and can't do.

The AEZ and Land Type maps say the same thing from a different angle — more than half the Haor sits in lowland or very lowland categories, where flood depth and duration leave no room for a second crop. Only the highland and piedmont edges, the ground that drains fast and dries early, get more than one shot at the calendar.

Flood management, land use planning, and crop scheduling in the Haor all run into the same constraint eventually. The water decides.

Exercise 01 — Thematic Mapping · Haor Region of Bangladesh Prepared by Deboneel Partho · CEGIS, RDT Division · ArcGIS Pro
